_meta.description: Track C bootstrap fixture: Housing Act 2004, Chapter 1 (HHSRS)
_meta.purpose: Ground truth for Gemma extraction validation. Every automated batch compares against this.
_meta.notes: Placeholder structure — populate during first Track C session with Lex MCP

provisions.title: Housing Act 2004, s.1 — New system for assessing housing conditions
Housing Act 2004, s.2 — Meaning of 'category 1 hazard' and 'category 2 hazard'
Housing Act 2004, s.3 — Operating provisions of this Part
Housing Act 2004, s.4 — Inspections by local housing authorities
Housing Act 2004, s.5 — Category 1 hazards: general duty to take enforcement action
Housing Act 2004, s.7 — Category 2 hazards: power to take enforcement action
Housing Act 2004, s.9 — Guidance about inspections and enforcement action

legal_nodes.statement: Local housing authority has a duty to take enforcement action in relation to category 1 hazards identified on inspection of residential premises.
Local housing authority has a power (but not a duty) to take enforcement action in relation to category 2 hazards.
legal_nodes.extraction_notes: s.7 creates a power, not a duty. This is the basis of the ENFORCEMENT_GAP finding: tenants with category 2 hazards have no enforceable right to council action.

legal_nodes.statement: Occupier of residential premises has a right to have the local housing authority take enforcement action where a category 1 hazard exists.
Improvement notice requiring landlord to remedy category 1 hazard within specified period (minimum 28 days).
Prohibition order preventing use of all or part of premises where category 1 hazard cannot be remedied by improvement.
Local housing authority must inspect residential premises if it has reason to believe a category 1 or category 2 hazard may exist (s.4 duty to inspect triggered by reasonable grounds).
legal_nodes.extraction_notes: Qualified: triggered by 'reason to believe', not unconditional.

legal_edges.explanation: The duty to act on category 1 hazards is enforced through improvement notices issued under s.11.
Where improvement is not feasible, the duty to act on category 1 hazards is enforced through prohibition orders under s.20.
The occupier's right to enforcement action is the correlative of the authority's duty under s.5.
Inspection duty (s.4) triggers enforcement duty (s.5) if category 1 hazard found.

cross_cutting_findings.description: Category 2 hazards create a power to act (s.7) but no duty. Tenants with category 2 hazards have no enforceable right to council action. This is a genuine legislative gap, not an extraction error.
POWER node for category 2 hazards has no corresponding LIABILITY node. Consistent with the ENFORCEMENT_GAP: because it is a power not a duty, there is no correlative liability on any party.
